concepts.lesson: Natural vegetation refers to plant communities that grow naturally without human aid and remain undisturbed for a long time. These plants develop according to the climate and soil of a region.
concepts.real_example: Dense forests of the Western Ghats grow naturally due to heavy rainfall.
key_points: Plants grow naturally without human help

concepts.lesson: Flora refers to the plant life of a region, while fauna refers to the animal life found in a particular area.
concepts.real_example: Teak trees are part of India's flora, while tigers are part of its fauna.

concepts.lesson: Tropical evergreen forests are dense forests found in regions receiving more than 200 cm of rainfall. Trees remain green throughout the year because they do not shed leaves at the same time.
concepts.real_example: These forests are found in the Western Ghats and Andaman and Nicobar Islands.
key_points: Important trees include ebony, mahogany and rosewood

concepts.lesson: Tropical deciduous forests are the most widespread forests in India. Trees shed their leaves during the dry season to conserve water.
concepts.real_example: Teak and sal trees are common in deciduous forests of central India.
key_points: Found in areas with moderate rainfall
Trees shed leaves in dry season
Common trees include teak, sal and sandalwood

concepts.lesson: Thorn forests develop in regions receiving less than 70 cm of rainfall. Plants here have long roots and small leaves to reduce water loss.
concepts.real_example: Thorn forests are common in Rajasthan and parts of Gujarat.
key_points: Includes acacia, cactus and thorny shrubs

concepts.lesson: Montane forests are found in mountainous regions where vegetation changes with altitude due to variation in temperature and rainfall.
concepts.real_example: Pine and deodar forests are found in the Himalayan region.

concepts.lesson: Mangrove forests are tidal forests found along coastal areas where rivers meet the sea. The plants grow in salty water and have roots that remain submerged.
concepts.real_example: The Sundarbans delta has extensive mangrove forests.
key_points: Grow in muddy and saline water

concepts.lesson: India has a rich variety of wildlife including mammals, birds, reptiles and fish. The diversity of vegetation supports a large number of animal species.
concepts.real_example: Elephants live in forests of Assam, Karnataka and Kerala.

concepts.lesson: Biodiversity is threatened due to human activities such as deforestation, pollution, hunting and introduction of alien species. These actions disturb ecological balance.
concepts.real_example: Excessive hunting and forest clearing have endangered many animal species.

concepts.lesson: Conservation refers to protecting plants and animals to maintain ecological balance. The government has introduced various measures such as biosphere reserves, national parks and wildlife sanctuaries.
concepts.real_example: Project Tiger was launched to protect the tiger population in India.
key_points: Establishment of national parks and sanctuaries

concepts.lesson: Biosphere reserves are protected areas created to conserve biodiversity and maintain natural ecosystems. They help protect both flora and fauna.
concepts.real_example: The Nilgiri Biosphere Reserve protects many plant and animal species.

concepts.lesson: Migratory birds travel from one region to another during different seasons in search of food and suitable climate.
concepts.real_example: Siberian cranes visit wetlands in India during winter.
key_points: Move in search of food and climate
